title: American Religion: Contemporary Trends By Mark Chaves Princeton University Press. 2011. 160 pages. $22.95 paper
abstract: This compact volume on contemporary trends in American religion by Mark Chaves may be an interesting place to start with students in sociology, history or political science. Against a backdrop of media hype about religious resurgence after 9/11 or religious decline on the heels of works like the Barna Group's UnChristian, Chaves's empirical treatment is a welcome corrective. Based largely on an analysis of the General Social Survey (GSS) from 1972 to 2008, the book summarizes continuity and change in American religious behavior and belief. Providing a counterweight to individual level data are insights from Chaves's own surveys of American congregations. The stated aim of the volume is to describe rather than interpret. And Chaves suggests a rule for what constitutes sound description: two data points should be considered “flimsy” evidence.(9) Fair enough, especially for data from the GSS. What is curious is the fact that Chaves's other primary data source is his National Congregations, which has been conducted only twice. The remedy, he suggests, is corroborating evidence. But I suspect that the target audience for the book, “those who do not know but want to know,” may be confused by the rule and unable to evaluate the consequences. Fortunately, Chaves is well-versed in his subject matter and certainly—as compared to other “broad brush” attempts in more polemical hands—the results do strike a balance between “the twin dangers of overstating and understating recent changes.”(3)
